---
title: "2001: A Quarto Odyssey"
subtitle: "I'm sorry Dave, Word can't do that"
author: "Tiago Jacinto — FMUP / RISE-Health"
date: "2026-07-03"
date-format: "D MMMM YYYY"
lang: en
format:
  html:
    theme: theme.scss
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    smooth-scroll: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    page-layout: article
    fig-align: center
    link-external-icon: false
    link-external-newwindow: true
    highlight-style: github
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
          <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin>
          <link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Inter:wght@400;500;600;700&family=Space+Grotesk:wght@500;600;700&family=JetBrains+Mono:wght@400;500&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
---

::: {.hero}
![](images/quartoodissey.png){alt="2001: A Quarto Odyssey — MEDCIDS Summer School 2026"}
:::

This course teaches you to use **Quarto** to integrate three activities you normally do in separate tools: managing references (Zotero), analysing data (R), and writing (Word, LaTeX, or another processor). By the end of the day, a single `.qmd` file produces `.docx`, PDF and HTML, with tables that update when the data change and citations that switch style in a second.

[Quarto, RStudio, Zotero, Better BibTeX — all *open source*, all cross-platform, all with versions for Windows, macOS and Linux.]{.column-margin}

You don't need to know R in depth — it's enough to load data, run functions and read the output. You don't need to have used Markdown or Quarto before.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Quick glossary — terms that show up right away

If any of these terms is new, read this box before continuing. More detailed definitions are in the [Glossary](#glossary) section below.

| Term                   | In one sentence                                                                    |
|------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Quarto**             | Scientific publishing system that brings text, code and references together in a single file. |
| **`.qmd`**             | Quarto's file extension. It's a text file with Markdown and code blocks.           |
| **Markdown**           | Lightweight markup language for writing formatted text (headings, lists, *italics*) without buttons. |
| **YAML**               | The "spec sheet" at the top of a `.qmd`: title, authors, output format, bibliography. |
| **R chunk**            | A block of R code inside the `.qmd`. When you render, the code runs and the output goes into the document. |
| **Render**             | The act of turning the `.qmd` into PDF, `.docx` or HTML.                            |
| **Zotero**             | Bibliographic reference manager. Stores authors, years, DOIs, PDFs.                |
| **Better BibTeX**      | Zotero extension that generates stable identifiers and keeps a `.bib` file up to date. |
| **`.bib`**             | Text file with all your references in BibTeX format. Quarto reads this file.       |
| **Cite key**           | Short identifier for a reference (`silva2024asthma`). It's what you write in the text. |
| **CSL**                | Citation Style Language. Defines how citations are formatted (Vancouver, APA, Nature). |
| **Pandoc**             | The engine that converts Markdown into PDF, `.docx` or HTML. Quarto uses Pandoc underneath. |
:::

# About the workshop

A health researcher does three things repeatedly: **manages references**, **analyses data** and **writes**. In most cases, each of these activities happens in a different tool that doesn't talk to the others — Zotero on one side, RStudio on another, Word on a third. The result is predictable: duplicated files, outdated references, tables copied by hand between programs, and formatting redone every time the document changes destination.

[The typical number of different versions of a manuscript in circulation during peer review: **5 to 8**. Each one a separate Word file. Version errors are one of the three most common causes of retractions due to incorrect data.]{.column-margin}

Quarto solves this problem. It's a scientific publishing system that lets you write text, insert citations managed by Zotero, and include blocks of R code that produce tables and figures directly in the final document. A change in the data propagates automatically to the tables and figures. A change of citation style applies in seconds. The same `.qmd` file generates a `.docx` to submit to a journal, a PDF to archive, and an HTML page to share.

This document serves two purposes. It's the **course script** you'll follow today. And it's the **reference material** you take with you: it lives in your project folder, so you can come back to it any time. Navigate the table of contents on the left to jump between sections. Each exercise has its own `README.md` in `exercises/` — that's where you'll do the hands-on work.

::: {.callout-tip}
## If you haven't opened the project yet

You run this course locally in **RStudio**. Get the materials (`git clone`, **Code → Download ZIP**, or **Use this template**), then open `quarto-odyssey.Rproj` in RStudio. The rest of this document assumes you have R, RStudio, Quarto and Zotero installed — see the [repository README](../README.md) for the four setup steps, and [tiagojct.eu/rsbp](https://tiagojct.eu/rsbp/) if RStudio is new to you.

No local install? The repo ships a `.devcontainer`, so you can open it in **GitHub Codespaces** as a fallback — everything below works there too.
:::

## What you'll be able to do by the end of the day

- Set up [Zotero](https://www.zotero.org) with [Better BibTeX](https://retorque.re/zotero-better-bibtex/) for stable *cite keys* and automatic export to `.bib`.
- Add references in five different ways: browser connector, DOI/PMID, PDF *drag-and-drop*, manual entry, `.bib`/`.ris` import.
- Check whether articles in your library have been **retracted** using [Retraction Watch](https://retractionwatch.com) and the alerts built into Zotero.
- Create a `.qmd` document with YAML *front matter*, Markdown text and blocks of R code.
- Integrate R code in a Quarto document to produce tables and figures that update automatically when the data change.
- Insert Zotero citations in the Quarto document and export to `.docx`, PDF and HTML with a formatted bibliography.
- Switch citation style (Vancouver, APA, Nature) in seconds, without manual editing.
- Recognise the [FAIR principles](https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles/) and understand how this workflow supports them.

# Word vs Quarto — a practical comparison

Before diving into the details, a side-by-side table of what changes when you move from Word to Quarto.

| Scenario                                 | Word                                                                            | Quarto                                                                    |
|------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Dataset updated, redo Table 1.           | Copy it again by hand from R / SPSS. Transcription errors are common.           | Re-render. The table updates on its own.                                  |
| Journal asks for Nature style not APA.   | Redo the whole bibliography by hand (1–3 hours for 50 references).              | Change one line in the YAML (`csl: nature.csl`). Re-render. 5 seconds.    |
| Co-author asks for PDF; journal for `.docx`. | Keep two files in sync, edit changes in parallel.                            | Same `.qmd`, generates both formats. Sync guaranteed.                     |
| Insert a new reference in the text.      | Word + Zotero plugin → choose → insert → renumber everything after it.          | Write `[@citekey]`. Automatic renumbering.                                |
| Reorganise sections (intro → end).       | Cut, paste, redo numbering, redo cross-references.                              | Change the order of the `#` in the `.qmd`. Cross-references resolve on their own. |
| Share the manuscript.                    | Attach `.docx` by email. Versions diverge quickly.                             | Share the Git repository. Everyone has the current version.               |
| Re-run the analysis two years from now.  | Hope you still have RStudio, the packages and the data. Often fails.            | `renv::restore()` rebuilds the environment. Render produces the same document. |

[Most tasks are still done in Word **out of inertia**, not superiority. The cost of learning Quarto pays off on the second submission to a journal that asks for a different style.]{.column-margin}

# Glossary

The core terms, in more detail:

**Quarto.** *Open source* scientific publishing system designed for researchers. Replaces Word, LaTeX and RMarkdown in a single ecosystem. Read more: [quarto.org](https://quarto.org).

[Developed by [Posit (formerly RStudio)](https://posit.co). Released in 2022, built on decades of learning from RMarkdown.]{.column-margin}

**`.qmd`.** Quarto's file extension. Underneath it's plain text, reads well in any editor, versions well with Git. It mixes prose (in Markdown), code (in R, Python, Julia or Observable) and metadata (in YAML).

**YAML.** The header at the top of the file, between `---` and `---`. It's where you set the title, authors, date, output format (`docx`, `pdf`, `html`), bibliography location, citation style. Indentation-sensitive syntax — spaces, not *tabs*.

[YAML = "YAML Ain't Markup Language". A recursive acronym, like GNU.]{.column-margin}

**Markdown.** Lightweight markup language. `# Title` is a level-1 heading. `**bold**` becomes bold. `[text](url)` is a link. Nothing else to learn on day one.

**R chunk.** A block of R code that runs when you render the `.qmd`. Marked between ` ```{r} ` and ` ``` `. The output (tables, figures, numeric values) goes into the final document. The execution options (`echo`, `eval`, `warning`, `message`, `label`) control what is shown.

**Render.** The act of turning the `.qmd` into a final document. In the terminal: `quarto render document.qmd`. In RStudio: the **Render** button. In VS Code with the Quarto extension: `Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + K`.

**Zotero.** *Open source* bibliographic reference manager. Stores metadata (authors, year, DOI), PDFs, annotations and notes. Has a desktop application and a synced online library. Read more: [zotero.org](https://www.zotero.org).

[Zotero was created in 2006 at [George Mason University](https://www.gmu.edu/) with funding from the Mellon Foundation. Today it's maintained by the Corporation for Digital Scholarship.]{.column-margin}

**Better BibTeX.** [Zotero extension](https://retorque.re/zotero-better-bibtex/) that does two critical things: it generates stable, readable identifiers (*cite keys*) (`silva2024asthma` instead of `gibberish123`), and it keeps an exported `.bib` file updated in real time.

**`.bib`.** Text file with all your references in [BibTeX format](https://www.bibtex.org/Format/). It's what Quarto reads to resolve your citations.

**Cite key.** Short identifier for a reference (`silva2024asthma`, `who2023air`). It's what you write in the text: `[@silva2024asthma]` produces `(Silva, 2024)` on render.

**CSL.** [Citation Style Language](https://citationstyles.org). Defines how each reference is formatted — Vancouver, APA 7, Nature, etc. There are over [10,000 ready-made styles](https://www.zotero.org/styles).

**Pandoc.** The [universal document conversion engine](https://pandoc.org). Quarto uses Pandoc underneath to generate `.docx`, PDF (via LaTeX), HTML and other formats.

[Pandoc was created by John MacFarlane, a philosopher at UC Berkeley. It's the Swiss army knife of document conversion: it accepts over 30 input formats and produces over 50 output formats.]{.column-margin}

**FAIR.** Acronym for *Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable*. [Principles](https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles/) for making data (and code, and documents) reusable in the long term. The Quarto + Zotero + R workflow supports all four.

# Why plain-text files?

Notice a pattern in that glossary: `.qmd`, `.bib`, `.csl`, `.yml`, `.csv` — they're all **plain text**. Not a coincidence. Every durable part of this workflow is a text file you can open in any editor, on any operating system, today or in twenty years. That single property is where most of the payoff comes from.

| File        | Holds                                  | Why it's text                                            |
|-------------|----------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| `.qmd`      | Your prose, code and metadata          | Editable anywhere; no proprietary format to go obsolete  |
| `.bib`      | Your references (BibTeX)               | Portable between tools; readable and fixable by hand      |
| `.csl`      | The citation style rules               | Swap one file, restyle the whole bibliography            |
| `.yml`      | Project/document settings              | Version-controllable configuration, not hidden in menus   |
| `.csv`      | Your data                              | Opens in R, Python, Excel, or a text editor — no lock-in  |

What plain text buys you:

- **Durability.** A `.docx` from 2005 can already be awkward to open. A text file never rots — the format is the content.
- **Diff-able history.** Because it's text, a version-control tool can show you *exactly which line changed* between two versions (more on this below). A binary `.docx` can only tell you "the file changed".
- **Tool independence.** RStudio today, Positron or VS Code tomorrow, a plain editor on a borrowed laptop — the files don't care.
- **Searchable and scriptable.** You can `grep` a folder of `.qmd` and `.bib` files, or write a script that touches a hundred of them. You cannot do that to a stack of Word documents.

[The opposite of plain text is a *binary* format — `.docx`, `.xlsx`, `.pdf` — where the bytes only make sense to the program that wrote them. Binary is fine for *output*; the trouble starts when it's also your *source*.]{.column-margin}

The rest of the course is, in a sense, just learning to work comfortably with a handful of well-chosen text files.

# The problem: three tools that don't talk to each other

Look at your typical workflow for writing an article:

1. You read PDFs and store them in Zotero (or Mendeley, or a `papers/` folder).
2. You analyse the dataset in R or SPSS, export tables and figures.
3. You open Word. You write the introduction. You copy the numbers from the R tables into the text. You insert the figures as images. You cite the papers from the Zotero/Mendeley plugin.

Every time a data point changes, you redo the analysis, copy the numbers again, paste the figures again. Every time the journal asks for another citation style, the bibliography is rebuilt by hand. Every time the supervisor asks for the file in PDF and the journal asks for `.docx`, you do two different exports from two different originals.

::: {.hal}
I'm sorry, Dave. I'm afraid I can't do that.

<cite>HAL 9000 · 1968</cite>
:::

HAL's line is what Word tells you, silently, every time you try to do something *reproducible* inside it: update a table without redoing it, change citation style without rewriting the bibliography, rebuild the document from the original data. Word wasn't built for that.

Quarto was.

# What is Quarto

[Quarto](https://quarto.org) is an *open source* scientific publishing system. Underneath, it brings together three things that already existed but lived apart:

- **Markdown** for the text.
- **Blocks of R code** (or Python, Julia, Observable) for analyses that run when you render.
- **`.bib` citations** managed externally by Zotero, inserted in the text with simple syntax.

When you render, Pandoc composes all of this into a final file — `.docx`, PDF or HTML, your choice. The tables come from the R code, the figures come from the R code, the citations come from the `.bib`. Change anything in one of the sources and re-render: the rest updates.

::: {.callout-note}
## Quarto vs RMarkdown

If you've used **RMarkdown**, Quarto is the broader successor. RMarkdown only runs R; Quarto runs R, Python, Julia, Observable. RMarkdown depends on R packages; Quarto is a standalone tool, installed on the system. Old `.Rmd` files run in Quarto with minimal changes — the [migration guide](https://quarto.org/docs/faq/rmarkdown.html) is short.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Quarto vs Jupyter Notebook

[Jupyter Notebooks](https://jupyter.org) are great for interactive exploration but poor for long-form writing. Quarto imports `.ipynb` directly, with the same chunk model. If you come from Python, you can use Quarto exactly as you used notebooks, with advantages in formatting, citations and export to `.docx`/PDF.
:::

# How to organise a Quarto project

Before you write a line of code, organise the folders. A simple structure that works for most projects:

```
my-article/
├── _quarto.yml          # Project configuration (optional for a single .qmd)
├── manuscript.qmd       # The main document
├── references.bib       # Bibliography exported from Zotero
├── data/
│   └── cohort-asma.csv  # Raw data
├── R/
│   └── analysis.R       # Reusable R functions (optional)
└── output/
    ├── manuscript.docx
    ├── manuscript.pdf
    └── manuscript.html
```

When the `.qmd` file reads data, use **relative paths** (`data/cohort-asma.csv`), not absolute ones. That way the project works on your machine, on your supervisor's, and anywhere else it's opened.

[Absolute path: `C:\Users\Tiago\Desktop\data.csv`. Relative path: `data/cohort.csv`. Only the second survives moving the project to another machine or to the *cloud*.]{.column-margin}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Five principles for a reproducible project

1. **One `.qmd` file per document.** Not five files sharing copied fragments.
2. **Relative paths always.** Never `C:\Users\Tiago\Desktop\data.csv`.
3. **Immutable raw data.** Don't overwrite the original `cohort-asma.csv`. Generate transformed files in `data-processed/` or in variables inside the `.qmd`.
4. **`renv` or Conda to pin versions.** R packages change; the `.qmd` that runs today may fail two years from now if you don't freeze the versions.
5. **Everything in Git.** The `.qmd` is text: Git reads it, sees *diffs*, recovers versions. Learn [Git in an hour](https://learngitbranching.js.org) and gain auditability for the rest of your life.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Synthetic data and real data

The `cohort-asma.csv` you'll use today is **synthetic** — generated for the exercise, with no connection to real patients. This lets us put the dataset in a GitHub repository (even a private one) with no risk whatsoever.

In your real projects, with patient data, different rules apply:

- **Never commit real patient data to a Git repository**, public or private. A repository can be shared, cloned, published by mistake, or *forked*; Git history keeps everything forever, even if you delete the file in a later commit. (The same applies to a Codespace built from the repository.)
- **Check your institution's data protection policy** (GDPR, ethics committee) before you even load patient data onto any machine — including your own laptop.
- **Confirm you have ethics committee approval** for the study in question before analysing the data.
- If you need to work with sensitive data in a Quarto workflow, keep it out of the repository (for example, in a `data/` folder listed in `.gitignore`, or in an institution-approved storage system) and use only relative paths to reference it locally.
:::

# Zotero and Better BibTeX

[Zotero](https://www.zotero.org) is the reference manager you'll use. It's *open source*, it's free, and it has a desktop application (Windows, macOS, Linux) plus sync with the *online* library. It solves a concrete problem: storing, organising and citing hundreds (or thousands) of articles without relying on PDF folders with names like `article_final_v2.pdf`.

Underneath, Zotero stores three things per reference: the **metadata** (authors, title, year, DOI — what goes into the bibliography), the **attachments** (the PDF, if you have access), and your **annotations** (highlights, notes). All of this syncs across computers and integrates directly with Word, LibreOffice, and — what matters here — with Quarto.

[For a fuller walkthrough of Zotero beyond what we cover today — collections, tags, group libraries, the built-in PDF reader — see [tiagojct.eu/zotero](https://tiagojct.eu/zotero).]{.column-margin}

::: {.callout-note}
## Zotero vs Mendeley vs EndNote

All three do the essentials (store references, insert citations). Zotero stands out on three points for a Quarto workflow: it's free with no storage limit for metadata (only PDFs count toward the quota), it has [Better BibTeX](https://retorque.re/zotero-better-bibtex/) — which none of the others replicates equivalently — and the [Retraction Watch Database](https://retractionwatch.com/retraction-watch-database/) has been built in since 2022. If you already use Mendeley or EndNote, [importing into Zotero](https://www.zotero.org/support/moving_to_zotero) is straightforward.
:::

## Five ways to add references

1. **Browser connector.** [Install the Zotero Connector](https://www.zotero.org/download/connectors) for Chrome, Firefox, Safari or Edge. You see an article on PubMed, click the Zotero icon in the browser bar, and the reference (plus the PDF, if there's access) goes into the library.
2. **DOI or PMID with the *magic wand*.** In Zotero desktop, click the magic wand button (top-left, with a green `+`). Paste a DOI (`10.1234/abc`), a PMID (`12345678`), or an ISBN. Zotero goes online to fetch the metadata.
3. ***Drag-and-drop* of a PDF.** Drag a PDF into the Zotero window. It tries to extract metadata automatically. It almost always works with modern scientific articles.
4. **Manual entry.** `Green + → Item Type` button. Useful for book chapters, conference papers, *grey literature* — things the other methods don't capture well.
5. **`.bib`/`.ris` import.** Have a bibliography inherited from another project? `File → Import` and select the file. The references go into a new collection.

[The connector works on journal sites, PubMed, Google Scholar, IEEE, ACM, Cochrane Library and over 600 others. See an updated list at [zotero.org/support/translators](https://www.zotero.org/support/translators).]{.column-margin}

::: {.callout-warning}
## Watch out for duplicates

Almost no method is duplicate-proof. Zotero detects obvious duplicates (same DOI), but variants (*preprint* version + published version) slip through. Before exporting the library, run `Edit → Find Duplicates` and merge the duplicates by hand.
:::

## Better BibTeX

[Better BibTeX](https://retorque.re/zotero-better-bibtex/) is a Zotero extension that does two critical things:

- **Stable, readable cite keys.** Instead of random identifiers, your reference becomes `silva2024asthma` — author, year, first meaningful word of the title. Configurable.
- **Automatic export to `.bib`.** You set it up once (`File → Export Library → Better BibTeX → Keep updated`) and the `.bib` file updates whenever you add a reference to Zotero.

Installation: download the `.xpi` from [retorque.re/zotero-better-bibtex/installation/](https://retorque.re/zotero-better-bibtex/installation/), and `Tools → Add-ons → Install Add-on From File`.

[Typical *cite key* pattern: `auth.lower + year + shorttitle(1,1).lower`. Produces `silva2024asthma`, `who2023air`. Configurable in **Preferences → Better BibTeX → Citation Key Format**.]{.column-margin}

## Retractions

Retracted articles keep being cited in the literature — including, regularly, by new articles whose authors did not check the status of their references. Citing retracted work is a serious integrity error.

[The best-known case in health is Wakefield 1998 — autism and the MMR vaccine. Retracted in 2010, it kept being cited hundreds of times a year to this day.]{.column-margin}

Since 2022, Zotero integrates the [Retraction Watch Database](https://retractionwatch.com/retraction-watch-database/). Whenever an article in your library is retracted, you see a red warning on the item's row. **Notice it before citing**.

::: {.callout-important}
## Non-negotiable rule

Before sending any manuscript for review, check for retractions two ways: the Zotero warning and a manual search on [Retraction Watch](http://retractiondatabase.org/RetractionSearch.aspx). If one of your references is retracted, decide whether to remove it (in most cases, yes) or to cite it explicitly as retracted (in cases where you're discussing the error).
:::

## Exercise 1 — Zotero library

Open [`exercises/01-zotero/README.md`](../exercises/01-zotero/README.md). You'll create a library with at least 10 references using three different methods, install and configure Better BibTeX, export to an automatic `.bib`, and check for retractions.

# YAML *front matter*

The *front matter* is the "spec sheet" at the top of the `.qmd`, between `---` and `---`:

```yaml
---
title: "Eosinophilic inflammation and lung function in children with asthma"
author:
  - name: Marta Silva
    email: marta.silva@chusj.min-saude.pt
    affiliations:
      - Centro Hospitalar Universitário de São João
  - name: Tiago Jacinto
    affiliations:
      - Faculty of Medicine, University of Porto
date: "2026-09-15"
abstract: |
  Eosinophilic inflammation is a dominant endotype of paediatric asthma...
bibliography: references.bib
csl: vancouver.csl
format:
  docx: default
  pdf: default
  html: default
---
```

YAML accepts hundreds of options. The ones you'll use most often:

- `title`, `subtitle`, `author`, `date` — basic metadata.
- `abstract` — the abstract, written in Markdown.
- `bibliography` — path to the `.bib`.
- `csl` — path to the CSL file with the citation style. If you omit it, Quarto uses the *Chicago author-date* style.
- `format` — output formats. You can have several.

The full *front matter* documentation is at [quarto.org/docs/reference/formats/html](https://quarto.org/docs/reference/formats/html.html) (and the equivalent for other formats).

[YAML also accepts authors with ORCID, multiple affiliations, and corresponding-author markers. See [quarto.org/docs/journals/authors.html](https://quarto.org/docs/journals/authors.html) for the full reference.]{.column-margin}

::: {.callout-warning}
## Mind the indentation

YAML is sensitive to spaces and indentation. Two spaces before `name:` inside `author:`. Never *tabs* — only spaces. If `quarto render` fails with a strange error, 80% of the time it's badly indented YAML.
:::

## YAML for a project, not just a document

When you have several `.qmd` files in the same project, you can define common options in a `_quarto.yml` file at the root:

```yaml
project:
  type: default

bibliography: references.bib
csl: vancouver.csl

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
  pdf:
    documentclass: scrartcl
    geometry: margin=2.5cm
```

Every `.qmd` in the folder inherits these options — you don't have to repeat them in each one.

# R code blocks

An R block (*code chunk*) is what makes Quarto different from Word: prose and code in the same file, code that runs when you render.

```{{r}}
#| label: tbl-demographics
#| tbl-cap: "Demographic characteristics of the sample"

library(tidyverse)
library(gt)

read_csv("data/cohort-asma.csv") |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    age_mean = mean(age),
    age_sd = sd(age)
  ) |>
  gt()
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Rusty R? Three things to recognise in this example

We won't teach R — but if you haven't touched it in a few months, this orients you for the exercises:

- **`|>` is the *pipe*.** Read it as "and then": it takes the result on the left and passes it as the first argument to the function on the right. `data |> summarise(...)` is the same as `summarise(data, ...)`, but reads top to bottom.
- **`summarise()` collapses rows into summaries** (mean, count). With `group_by(sex)` before it, it produces a summary *per group*.
- **`gt()` turns the table into a formatted object** for the final document.

If you need more, [R for Data Science (2e)](https://r4ds.hadley.nz) (free) covers this in chapters 3–4.
:::

The `#|` options (called *hash-pipe options*) control the chunk's behaviour:

| Option       | What it does                                               |
|--------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| `label`      | Unique identifier, used for *cross-references*.            |
| `tbl-cap` / `fig-cap` | Caption of the table or figure.                   |
| `echo`       | Show the code in the final document? (`true`/`false`)      |
| `eval`       | Run the code? If `false`, shows the code but doesn't run it. |
| `warning`    | Show R's warnings?                                         |
| `message`    | Show messages (e.g. packages loaded)?                     |
| `include`    | Include the output in the document? `false` runs but hides it. |
| `fig-width` / `fig-height` | Figure dimensions in inches.                  |
| `cache`      | Store the result on disk. Useful for long chunks.         |

[The full list of chunk options is at [quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-knitr.html](https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-knitr.html).]{.column-margin}

For a clean manuscript, you usually want `echo: false`, `warning: false`, `message: false` on every analysis chunk — so the final text shows only tables, figures and numbers, with no code or noise. You can set these options globally in the YAML, with:

```yaml
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## When to show code?

In manuscripts, always hide it — `echo: false`. In teaching notes or methodological supplements, show it — `echo: true`. In internal reports with colleagues who can read R, consider showing it — code is the most honest documentation of the analysis.
:::

## Five principles for readable chunks

1. **One chunk per task.** Don't mix data loading, transformation and visualisation in the same block.
2. **`label` on every chunk.** Even if you won't reference them — it makes debugging easier when something fails.
3. **Descriptive names.** `tbl-demographics` is better than `chunk-1`.
4. **Separate I/O from processing.** Read the dataset once at the start, in its own chunk. Transformations use the loaded object.
5. **Comments where the logic isn't obvious.** The `tidyverse` is readable on its own, but clinical decisions (e.g. "we excluded patients with FEV1 < 0.5 L because...") deserve a line of comment.

## *Cross-references*

When you give `label: tbl-demographics` to a table and `label: fig-spirometry` to a figure, you can reference them in the text with `@tbl-demographics` and `@fig-spirometry`. Quarto resolves the numbering automatically — you don't have to update "Table 1" to "Table 2" when you insert a new table.

Quarto convention: `tbl-*` for tables, `fig-*` for figures, `eq-*` for equations, `sec-*` for sections, `lst-*` for code listings. Quarto numbers them by type.

[Full cross-reference syntax: [quarto.org/docs/authoring/cross-references.html](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/cross-references.html). Includes references to sub-figures (`@fig-spirometry-1`), combined references (`@tbl-a; @tbl-b`), and more.]{.column-margin}

## Tables with `gt` and `knitr::kable()`

For basic tables, [`knitr::kable()`](https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown-cookbook/kable.html) is enough:

```{{r}}
mtcars |>
  head() |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "First six cars")
```

For tables with serious formatting (grouped headers, coloured cells, totals), [`gt`](https://gt.rstudio.com) is the right package. A steeper learning curve, but a *publication-ready* result.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Other table options

- [`gtsummary`](https://www.danieldsjoberg.com/gtsummary/) — publication-ready statistical tables (demographic Table 1, regression models, etc.).
- [`flextable`](https://davidgohel.github.io/flextable/) — alternative to `gt`, with better Word support.
- [`kableExtra`](https://haozhu233.github.io/kableExtra/) — adds advanced formatting to `knitr::kable()`.
:::

## Figures with `ggplot2`

[`ggplot2`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org) is the standard for visualisation in R. It's worth investing time in it once in your life. Recommendation: the book [R for Data Science (2e)](https://r4ds.hadley.nz), chapters 2 and 9–11.

Minimal example of a figure for a manuscript:

```{{r}}
#| label: fig-fev1-age
#| fig-cap: "FEV1 as a function of age, stratified by severity"
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5

ggplot(cohort, aes(x = age, y = fev1_l, colour = asthma_severity)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(x = "Age (years)", y = "FEV1 (L)", colour = "Severity") +
  theme_minimal()
```

[For *publication-ready* figures, consider the packages [`ggpubr`](https://rpkgs.datanovia.com/ggpubr/) (for statistical annotations) and [`patchwork`](https://patchwork.data-imaginist.com) (for combining multiple plots).]{.column-margin}

## Exercise 2 — Configure the analysis document

Open [`exercises/02-quarto-analysis/README.md`](../exercises/02-quarto-analysis/README.md). You get a `.qmd` whose R code **already works** — you don't write code. Your job is the Quarto layer: hide the code (`echo`), give the table and figure a label and a caption, size the figure, and add the cross-references. Re-render after each change and watch the effect.

# Markdown in Quarto

Everything that isn't YAML or R code is Markdown. The syntax you'll use almost always:

| Syntax                        | Renders as                                  |
|-------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| `# Heading`                   | Level-1 heading (h1)                        |
| `## Heading`                  | Level-2 heading (h2)                        |
| `**bold**`                    | **bold**                                    |
| `*italic*`                    | *italic*                                    |
| `` `inline code` ``           | `inline code`                               |
| `- item`                      | Unordered list item                         |
| `1. item`                     | Ordered list item                           |
| `[text](https://url)`         | Link                                        |
| `![alt](image.png)`           | Image                                       |
| `> quote`                     | Block quote                                 |
| `---`                         | Horizontal rule                             |
| `$E = mc^2$`                  | Inline equation (LaTeX)                      |
| `$$ \int x^2 dx $$`           | Block equation                              |

For the rest (manual tables, footnotes, cross-references, *callouts*), the [Quarto Markdown reference](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html) has everything in one place.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Markdown *cheat sheet*

To print and keep handy: the [Quarto Markdown cheatsheet](https://rstudio.github.io/cheatsheets/html/quarto.html) fits on one A4 page and covers 90% of what you'll use.
:::

## Callouts — highlight boxes in the text

Quarto supports five types of *callouts* — boxes with their own colour and icon to draw attention. The syntax is simple:

```markdown
::: {.callout-note}
## Callout title
Content in Markdown.
:::
```

Available types: `note`, `tip`, `warning`, `important`, `caution`. Each with its own colour and semantics — use them deliberately: `tip` for practical advice, `warning` for warnings, `important` for absolute rules.

You can also add `collapse="true"` to make the *callout* expandable, or `appearance="simple"` for a more discreet version.

## Margin notes — notes in the side column

Anything you place in a `div` with the class `.column-margin` appears in the right margin of the document, instead of in the main flow. Useful for definitions, secondary references, or author comments.

[This box you're reading now — it's a *margin note*. It draws the eye without interrupting linear reading.]{.column-margin}

```markdown
::: {.column-margin}
This is a margin note.
:::
```

Or inline with the syntax `[text]{.column-margin}`.

# Citations in Quarto

Once you have a `.bib` file exported from Zotero and its path declared in the YAML (`bibliography: references.bib`), inserting citations is trivial:

| Syntax in the `.qmd`        | Renders (*author-date* style)       | Renders (Vancouver)   |
|-----------------------------|-------------------------------------|-----------------------|
| `@silva2024asthma`          | Silva (2024)                        | 1                     |
| `[@silva2024asthma]`        | (Silva, 2024)                       | (1)                   |
| `[-@silva2024asthma]`       | 2024                                | 1                     |
| `[@silva2024asthma; @ho2023]` | (Silva, 2024; Ho, 2023)           | (1, 2)                |
| `[@silva2024asthma, p. 23]` | (Silva, 2024, p. 23)                | (1, p. 23)            |
| `[see @silva2024asthma]`    | (see Silva, 2024)                   | (see 1)               |

The final bibliography appears automatically at the end of the document, formatted according to the CSL you chose.

## The RStudio citation picker

If you'd rather not memorise *cite keys*, in RStudio 2022.07+ you can use the visual citation picker: `Insert → Citation` or `Ctrl + Shift + F8` (on Mac, `Cmd + Shift + F8`). You search by author or title, and RStudio inserts the correct `@citekey`.

In VS Code with the [Quarto extension](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=quarto.quarto), the equivalent shortcut is `@` in the editor — it opens a filtered list of your references.

## Switching style

To switch from Vancouver to APA, or to the *Thorax* journal style, you download the corresponding `.csl` file from [zotero.org/styles](https://www.zotero.org/styles), save it in the project, and change the line in the YAML:

```yaml
csl: thorax.csl
```

Re-render, done. Without rewriting a single citation.

[The CSL Project repository has over 10,000 styles. For most health journals you'll find the right style by searching the journal name at [zotero.org/styles](https://www.zotero.org/styles).]{.column-margin}

# Export to multiple formats

Quarto's magic is in three lines of YAML:

```yaml
format:
  docx: default
  pdf: default
  html: default
```

When you render, all three files are generated. For journals that require `.docx`, you have it. For institutional archiving, you have PDF. For online sharing (preprint, personal page, GitHub Pages), you have HTML.

[There's a fourth format gaining ground: `typst`. It generates PDF without LaTeX, much faster, and the first render doesn't download packages. It's still recent, but for reports and notes it's worth trying `format: typst`.]{.column-margin}

Each format can have its own options:

```yaml
format:
  docx:
    reference-doc: journal-X-template.docx
  pdf:
    documentclass: scrartcl
    fontsize: 11pt
    geometry: margin=2.5cm
  html:
    toc: true
    theme: cosmo
```

The [full options reference](https://quarto.org/docs/reference/) is organised by format.

::: {.callout-note}
## Journal templates for Quarto

Several journals already have official Quarto templates: [PLOS One](https://github.com/quarto-journals/plos), [American Statistical Association](https://github.com/quarto-journals/jasa), [Elsevier](https://github.com/quarto-journals/elsevier). The list keeps growing. Search [github.com/quarto-journals](https://github.com/quarto-journals) before making your own template.

To use a template (example with PLOS):

```bash
quarto use template quarto-journals/plos
```

It creates the structure ready to fill in.
:::

## Format-specific gotchas

::: {.callout-warning}
## `.docx` — where it fails

- Complex `gt` tables lose formatting. For Word, prefer `flextable` (which has native support).
- Vector images (SVG, PDF) can become pixelated. For Word, export figures as high-resolution PNG.
- Numbered headings need `number-sections: true` in the YAML.
- LaTeX equations don't always render well in Word. For complex formulas, consider including them as an image.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## PDF — where it fails

- The first render is slow because `tinytex` installs missing LaTeX packages. The following renders are fast.
- Non-ASCII characters in YAML metadata sometimes need escaping. If you see a font error, adding `mainfont: "Latin Modern Roman"` to the YAML usually fixes it.
- Tables that exceed the page width are a classic problem. Solution: `quarto/scale-down` or rotation to *landscape*.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## HTML — where it fails

- Very wide tables break alignment. Solution: `tbl-colwidths: [20, 30, 50]` to force widths.
- High-resolution images make the HTML heavy. Consider `optimize-html: true` in the YAML.
- To share offline, use `embed-resources: true` to bundle CSS, JS and images inside the `.html`.
:::

## Exercise 3 — Format, cite and export

Open [`exercises/03-writing-export/README.md`](../exercises/03-writing-export/README.md). The Introduction and Methods are **already written** — you don't write prose. You format the given text (headings, a list, a callout, emphasis), insert citations at the marked spots, add the cross-references, and export the document to `.docx`, PDF and HTML — then switch citation style in one line.

# Post-mortem — common errors when rendering

When `quarto render` fails, the error message is usually honest but verbose. Six patterns cover most cases.

::: {.callout-warning}
## "Error: cannot find object" or "object 'x' not found"

A chunk uses a variable that wasn't created. Check that the setup chunk (which creates `cohort` or `data`) is before the chunk that uses it, and that it ran without errors.

If you're using `quarto preview`, edit the setup chunk, save, and re-run all chunks with **Run All** in RStudio or `Ctrl + Shift + Enter` in VS Code.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## "Error: There is no package called 'X'"

The R package isn't installed. In RStudio, open the **Terminal** tab (or use the R **Console**) and run:

```r
install.packages("X")
```

For rarer packages, you may need to specify the repository:

```r
install.packages("X", repos = "https://cran.rstudio.com")
```
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## "! LaTeX Error: File 'X.sty' not found"

An error only in PDF renders. `tinytex` installs missing packages on the first render. If it fails, force the installation:

```r
tinytex::tlmgr_install("X")
```

If it persists, run `tinytex::reinstall_tinytex()` to reinstall the whole LaTeX distribution (takes 5–10 min).
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## "Citation @citekey not found in references.bib"

The cite key you wrote doesn't exist in the `.bib`. Common causes:

1. A typo in the `.qmd` — `@silvva2024` instead of `@silva2024`.
2. The reference hasn't been exported from Zotero yet — check that Better BibTeX has **Keep updated** active.
3. The `.bib` is at a different path than the YAML points to.

For all 3 cases, open the `.bib` in an editor and search for the key. If it's not there, copy an existing key and see how it's spelled.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## "YAML parse error" — strange syntax

YAML is sensitive to indentation and quotes. Four frequent causes:

1. *Tabs* instead of spaces — YAML rejects *tabs*.
2. Missing space after the `:` — `title:foo` doesn't work, `title: foo` does.
3. Malformed smart quotes — use classic `"`, not `"` or `"`.
4. Inconsistent indentation — always use 2 spaces, never 4 in one section and 2 in another.

If the YAML is long, validate it at [yamllint.com](https://www.yamllint.com) before re-rendering.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## "Misplaced alignment tab character &" — special characters in PDF

Only in PDF renders. The characters `&`, `%`, `_`, `#`, `$` have their own meaning in LaTeX. A raw `&` in the text (e.g. "Health & Technology"), in a caption, or in a `.bib` field makes the PDF render fail.

- **In your text:** escape them with a backslash — `\&`, `\%`, `\_`, `\#`, `\$`.
- **In the `.bib`:** let Better BibTeX handle this — re-export the library instead of editing the `.bib` by hand.

Accented characters (á, ç, ã, õ) are **not** the problem: the default engine (LuaLaTeX/XeLaTeX) handles them natively. If you see garbled characters (`Ã§` instead of `ç`), check that you didn't force `pdf-engine: pdflatex` in the YAML — the old `pdflatex` doesn't handle UTF-8 well.
:::

# Typical profiles — what's your starting point?

::: {.callout-note}
## I'm a **PhD student** starting my thesis

Recommended:

1. Do the full course (Zotero + Quarto + R chunks + export).
2. For the thesis itself, consider the [Quarto thesis extension](https://github.com/nmfs-opensci/quarto-thesis) or look for your university's specific template in the [curated list of Quarto thesis templates](https://github.com/Jupyter4Science/awesome-quarto-thesis).
3. Learn **Git** in parallel (1h at [learngitbranching.js.org](https://learngitbranching.js.org)) — you'll thank yourself by the fifth draft of the introduction.
4. Sync the Zotero library across three places: laptop, lab machine, online account. Make a monthly *backup* of the `.bib` to the project's Git repository.
5. Consider writing the thesis in [Quarto Book](https://quarto.org/docs/books/) format — separate chapters in a single project, with a shared bibliography.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## I'm a **researcher submitting to a specific journal**

Recommended:

1. Check whether the journal has an official Quarto template at [github.com/quarto-journals](https://github.com/quarto-journals).
2. Otherwise, find the journal's **CSL** at [zotero.org/styles](https://www.zotero.org/styles). Save it in the project.
3. Set up the YAML to export `.docx` (which is what most journals still accept) — `format: docx: default`.
4. Keep the `.qmd` + `data/` + `references.bib` files in a public Git repository (Zenodo, OSF, GitHub) **if the journal's policy allows it**. It increases the odds of acceptance at journals with reproducibility requirements (Lancet, BMJ, several Nature journals).
5. For journals with strict reproducibility requirements (Nature Code Sharing Policy, BMJ Open Data), include a `Code availability` statement in the manuscript pointing to your repository.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## I'm a **teacher** preparing teaching materials

Recommended:

1. Quarto is excellent for class handouts that mix theory and code running in real time.
2. Consider the `revealjs` format for slides — the same `.qmd` can produce slides for class and PDF notes for students.
3. For a full course, consider the [Quarto Book](https://quarto.org/docs/books/) or [Quarto Website](https://quarto.org/docs/websites/) format, with multiple pages and navigation.
4. Publish online with [Quarto Pub](https://quartopub.com) (free), GitHub Pages, or Netlify.
5. For interactive R exercises without students needing to install anything, [Webr](https://docs.r-wasm.org/webr/) runs R in the browser itself.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## I already use **LaTeX + BibTeX** for everything

Good news: the transition is smooth.

1. Your `.bib` already works, unchanged.
2. LaTeX equations (`$$...$$`) keep working inside Quarto.
3. For sophisticated PDFs, Quarto uses LaTeX underneath — you can inject `\usepackage{...}` in the preamble via YAML.
4. In exchange, you gain the ability to export to `.docx` (for co-authors who insist on Word) and HTML (for quick sharing).
5. For those coming from Sweave/knitr, the transition is practically nil.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## I already use **Word + Mendeley** and the system "works"

Recommended:

1. Migrate the library from Mendeley to Zotero ([official guide](https://www.zotero.org/support/kb/mendeley_import)). Zotero is *open source* and independent of any publisher; Mendeley belongs to Elsevier, which has already discontinued (and then walked back) parts of the product — a history worth considering before relying on it long term.
2. Start by using Quarto **only** for documents where code and data are part of the output (quantitative articles, analytical reports). Keep Word for purely textual documents during the transition.
3. As you gain confidence, expand. There's no prize for adopting everything at once.
4. Keep Word available for co-authors who insist — export to `.docx` when needed, but your original stays `.qmd`.
:::

# FAIR principles

[FAIR](https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles/) is the acronym for *Findable*, *Accessible*, *Interoperable*, *Reusable* — principles for data (and code, and documents) reusable in the long term. The Quarto + Zotero + R workflow supports all four:

- **Findable.** Persistent identifiers in the references (DOIs); explicit metadata in the YAML.
- **Accessible.** The `.qmd` is plain text, readable in any editor; the data are in open formats (CSV, JSON); the output formats are standard (`.docx`, PDF, HTML).
- **Interoperable.** Markdown, BibTeX and CSL are open formats; you can switch tools without losing the work.
- **Reusable.** The same `.qmd` re-renders with updated data; someone else who opens the repository can reproduce the document; the code is auditable.

[The technical literature on FAIR started with [Wilkinson et al. 2016 — *Nature Scientific Data*](https://doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2016.18). Worth reading if you work on projects with a data-sharing component.]{.column-margin}

# Version control, in brief

You don't need version control to finish today's exercises. But the moment your files are plain text — which, after the last few sections, they are — it becomes the single most valuable habit to add next. Here's the idea in one minute.

**The problem it solves.** You've seen the folder that grows `thesis.docx`, `thesis_v2.docx`, `thesis_final.docx`, `thesis_final_REALLY.docx`. Version control replaces that with **one file and a labelled history**: instead of copies, you take *named snapshots* ("added subgroup analysis") that you can return to at any time.

**Git vs GitHub.** Two different things people often confuse:

- **Git** is the tool that runs on *your computer* and records those snapshots. It works offline, on any files, and — because your sources are plain text — it can show you the exact line that changed between any two versions (`git diff`), not just "the file changed".
- **GitHub** (and GitLab, Codeberg, and others) is a *website* that hosts a copy of your Git history online — for backup, for sharing, and for collaborating without emailing files around. You've already used one: this course lives in a GitHub repository.

**Why it clicks with this workflow.** A `.qmd` project is exactly what version control was built for: small text files, meaningful line-level changes, one canonical version instead of a swarm of copies. Data, code, references and prose all get an auditable history for free.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Learn it properly, in the right order

I've written two short, health-researcher-focused guides that go beyond this section:

- [Git for health researchers: version control from day one](https://tiagojct.eu/git) — the concepts and the everyday commands.
- [GitHub for health researchers: sharing, collaborating, and open science](https://tiagojct.eu/github) — hosting, collaboration, and reproducibility.

For a hands-on, visual feel of branching, [Learn Git Branching](https://learngitbranching.js.org) is 60 well-spent minutes.
:::

The whole everyday loop is really five commands:

[Git works like Word's track-changes history ("go back to yesterday's version"), but for any file, with explicit labels. Indispensable with more than one collaborator, valuable even solo.]{.column-margin}

```bash
# 1. Initialise a repository in your project
cd my-project/
git init

# 2. Tell Git who you are (once per machine)
git config user.email "tiago@example.com"
git config user.name "Tiago Jacinto"

# 3. Add everything and take the first snapshot
git add .
git commit -m "Initial version"

# 4. Put it on GitHub (via the gh CLI)
gh repo create my-project --private --source=. --push

# 5. From now on, every time you change something meaningful
git add .
git commit -m "Add subgroup analysis"
git push
```

Go back at any time with `git log` (see the history) and `git checkout <hash>` (return to an old snapshot). That's 90% of what you'll ever do.

# Next steps

What you learned today covers 80% of the tasks. For the other 20%:

- **Version control with Git** (see the section above). The single highest-value habit to add once you're comfortable — start with [tiagojct.eu/git](https://tiagojct.eu/git).
- **`renv` to pin R packages.** R packages change version; a `.qmd` that runs today may fail two years from now if the `tidyverse` changed. [`renv`](https://rstudio.github.io/renv/) freezes the project's package versions. Analogous to `requirements.txt` in Python.
- **[Positron](https://positron.posit.co)**, the successor to RStudio. In late *beta*. Same team, new codebase, based on VS Code. Worth trying once you're comfortable with RStudio.
- **Quarto extensions.** There are [many](https://quarto.org/docs/extensions/) — for slides, posters, *journal articles*, *flashcards*. Search before making your own.
- **Citations with [Citation File Format (CFF)](https://citation-file-format.github.io)**, to make your own code citable.

## Mini-tutorial — `renv` in 3 commands

```r
# 1. Activate renv in the project
renv::init()

# 2. Whenever you install a new package
renv::snapshot()

# 3. When you open the project on another machine
renv::restore()
```

`renv` creates a `renv/` folder and a `renv.lock` file with the exact package versions. Commit both to Git. On any machine, `renv::restore()` rebuilds the environment.

# Resources

To go deeper after the course:

**Quarto:**

- [Official Quarto guide](https://quarto.org/docs/guide/) — full reference.
- [Quarto for academics](https://quarto.org/docs/manuscripts/) — section dedicated to scientific manuscripts.
- [Quarto Discussions](https://github.com/quarto-dev/quarto-cli/discussions) — official forum, active community.
- [Awesome Quarto](https://github.com/mcanouil/awesome-quarto) — curated list of extensions, examples, templates.

**R, tidyverse, ggplot2:**

- [R for Data Science (2e)](https://r4ds.hadley.nz) — essential manual, free.
- [ggplot2 cheatsheet](https://rstudio.github.io/cheatsheets/data-visualization.pdf).
- [gt documentation](https://gt.rstudio.com).
- [The Epidemiologist R Handbook](https://epirhandbook.com) — manual focused on public health.

**Zotero and Better BibTeX:**

- [Zotero documentation](https://www.zotero.org/support/).
- [Better BibTeX manual](https://retorque.re/zotero-better-bibtex/).
- [Using Zotero (Tiago Jacinto)](https://tiagojct.eu/zotero) — tutorial in English.
- [Zettlr and Zotero: a plain-text workflow for scientific writing](https://tiagojct.eu/zettlr-zotero).

**Reproducibility:**

- [The Turing Way](https://the-turing-way.netlify.app) — guide to reproducibility in research.
- [Wilkinson et al. 2016 — FAIR Guiding Principles](https://doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2016.18).
- [Goodman et al. 2016 — What does research reproducibility mean?](https://doi.org/10.1126/scitranslmed.aaf5027).

**Related tutorials (Tiago Jacinto):**

- [RStudio: Best practices for scientific work](https://tiagojct.eu/rsbp/).
- [Computing Foundations for Health Researchers](https://tiagojct.eu/computing-foundations).

The project you set up today is yours. You can come back to it whenever you want, and use it as a starting point for your own work. Take everything from here.

# Closing

You leave here with a workflow that brings the three core activities together — managing references, analysing data, writing — in a single reproducible project. No isolated tool does this better. Take the workflow with you, and adapt it to your work.

# Why "2001: A Quarto Odyssey"?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## For those who enjoyed the joke (and those who never saw the film)

The course name is a pun on *[2001: A Space Odyssey](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2001:_A_Space_Odyssey_(film))* — Stanley Kubrick's 1968 film (screenplay co-written with Arthur C. Clarke) about a space mission to Jupiter, commanded by two astronauts and an onboard computer called **HAL 9000**. HAL fails and the mission goes wrong. It's a landmark of science-fiction cinema and of reflection on artificial intelligence — fitting, indirectly, for a course about tools that replace Word.

The subtitle, **"I'm sorry Dave, Word can't do that"**, rewrites the film's most famous line: *"I'm sorry, Dave. I'm afraid I can't do that."* HAL says this to David Bowman, the astronaut, when he asks it to open the ship's doors. In the context of the course, it's what Word tells you, silently, every time you try to do something *reproducible* inside it.

The poster image is the **monolith** — a rectangular, perfectly smooth, black object that appears in the film at key moments of human evolution. It works as a metaphor for a tool that changes how we think. The red dot in front of the monolith is HAL's eye, always watching.

Other references from the film you might come across in conversations about Quarto and reproducibility:

**"Open the pod bay doors, HAL."** Bowman to HAL, asking to enter the ship. In prompting and tool-instruction contexts, it's cited as an example of the "clear request that needs a clear answer".

**"Just what do you think you're doing, Dave?"** HAL to Bowman when he tries to shut it down. In AI-output-verification contexts, it's used as a reminder that tools that appear to cooperate may be running their own agenda.

**The *Star Child*** — the film's final figure, the birth of a new consciousness after contact with the monolith. Metaphorically, the researcher who leaves a fragmented workflow for an integrated, reproducible one.

If you've never seen the film, none of this is needed to take the course. But it's a classic — and it goes down well after learning to use a tool with this name.
:::

---

Tiago Jacinto · FMUP / RISE-Health · MEDCIDS Summer School 2026
